title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
text:
Why prompting is the skill
Every generative tool you use takes a prompt. The specifics vary; the underlying skill transfers.

Image tools, video tools, audio tools, coding tools — all prompt-driven.
A filmmaker who prompts well in one tool prompts better in the next on day one.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
text:
Specificity beats cleverness
“A cinematic shot” gives the model permission to do anything.
“A medium shot, 35mm, golden-hour backlight, shallow depth of field, subject in profile” narrows the space.
Narrow spaces produce useful outputs.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
section: Tell it what, not how — unless the how matters
text:
Tell it what, not how — unless the how matters

For open creative work: describe the outcome. Let the model choose the approach.
For technical work: describe the approach. The outcome depends on it.

Rule of thumb: the more downstream work depends on the details, the more you specify them.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
text:
Show, don’t only tell
Reference images, code examples, prior outputs — usually teach the model faster than adjectives.
Modern tools accept multiple modalities of reference. Use them.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
text:
Constrain to create
Counter-intuitively: adding constraints improves output.

A time budget.
A palette.
A rule: “no text in image.”

Constraints give the model edges to push against.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
text:
Iterate in small moves
Change one thing per attempt.
Rewrite half the prompt between generations and you won’t know which change helped.
Same discipline as debugging code.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
section: The prompt is not the whole context
text:
The prompt is not the whole context
In media: seeds, samplers, reference images shape the output alongside the prompt.
In code: project files, CLAUDE.md, conversation history shape it.
The prompt is one input among several.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
text:
Common mistakes

Prompt-and-pray. One long prompt, disappointed, completely different long prompt. Iterate on a working base instead.
Over-stuffing adjectives. “Epic, cinematic, masterpiece, 8K” usually adds noise, not signal.
Ignoring the tool’s own prompting guide. Every major tool publishes one. Read it.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
text:
A tiny exercise
Pick a tool you’ve used. Write three prompts for the same outcome:

Vague.
Specific.
Specific with constraints.

Compare results. This recalibrates what “specific” actually means faster than any list of tips.

title: Prompting Principles — An Introduction
section: A deeper look at constraint stacking
text:
A deeper look at constraint stacking
When a prompt feels “done” but results are middling, add one more constraint — not more description.
Example progression for a single shot:

A street at night.
A street at night, 35mm, low angle.
A street at night, 35mm, low angle, wet pavement, neon signage only as light source.
A street at night, 35mm, low angle, wet pavement, neon signage only as light source, no visible pedestrians.

Each step narrows. Each step improves. Notice that #4’s constraint is subtractive — removing a default.
